title: File I/O
summary: Read from and write to files using Python's built-in file handling with context managers

sections.title: Files are the program's memory between runs
sections.content: Variables disappear when your program ends — like writing notes on a whiteboard and then erasing them. Files are like a notebook where you can store information permanently. Python makes file handling straightforward: you open a file, read or write its contents, then close it. The 'with' statement (context manager) is the recommended approach — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs, just like a responsible hiker who always closes the campsite food container to keep bears out. Files can be read as text (strings) or binary (bytes).

sections.title: Writing and reading a configuration file
sections.content: Write settings to a file, then read them back
sections.code:
# Configuration settings for an application
config = {
    "theme": "dark",  # UI color scheme preference
    "language": "en",  # Language code for localization
    "font_size": 14  # Base font size in pixels
}

# Write each config key-value pair to a file
with open("config.txt", "w") as file:  # "w" mode creates/overwrites the file
    for key, value in config.items():  # Iterate over dictionary items
        # Write each setting as "key=value" followed by a newline
        file.write(f"{key}={value}\n")  # \n starts a new line
    # File is automatically closed when the 'with' block ends

# Now read the file back and reconstruct the dictionary
loaded_config = {}  # Start with an empty dictionary
with open("config.txt", "r") as file:  # "r" mode opens for reading
    for line in file:  # Iterates through the file line by line
        # Strip whitespace and split on "=" — unpack into two variables
        key, value = line.strip().split("=")  # "theme=dark" -> ["theme", "dark"]
        # Store in the dictionary (value stays as string for now)
        loaded_config[key] = value

print(loaded_config)
# Expected output:
# {'theme': 'dark', 'language': 'en', 'font_size': '14'}

sections.title: File modes and best practices
sections.content: The open() function's mode determines what you can do: 'r' for reading (default), 'w' for writing (overwrites existing content), 'a' for appending (adds to end), 'r+' for reading and writing. Always use 'with open(...) as file:' — it's the context manager that guarantees the file handle is closed, even if an exception occurs. Never call .read() on entire files without checking size first — large files (GBs) can exhaust memory. Instead, iterate line by line or read in chunks. Use 'b' with the mode for binary files (images, executables): 'rb', 'wb'.

sections.title: Forgetting to close files causes data loss
sections.content: Writing without 'with' and forgetting .close() may not actually save your data to disk. Python buffers writes in memory and flushes when the file is closed. Wrong: f = open('data.txt', 'w'); f.write('hello') — if the program crashes before f.close(), the file is empty. Correct: use 'with open('data.txt', 'w') as f: f.write('hello')' — it closes automatically. Also, 'w' mode overwrites silently — use 'x' mode to create a new file (raises FileExistsError if it already exists) when you want to avoid accidental overwrites.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a program that reads a file called 'scores.txt' where each line contains a student name and a score separated by a comma (e.g., 'Alice,85'). Read all lines, calculate the average score, and write the average to a new file called 'average.txt' in the format 'Average score: 82.5'. Create the scores.txt file yourself with 5 students first.
hints: What happens if the file doesn't exist when you try to open it?
How do you split each line by comma to get the name and score separately?
